entries.name: ALTER
entries.purpose: Modify a GO TO target at runtime. ALTER P-030 TO PROCEED TO P-040 changes where GO TO in P-030 jumps.
entries.mainframe_context: Before EVALUATE (COBOL-85), ALTER was the standard way to implement computed dispatch. IBM training courses taught it as an 'advanced technique'.
entries.modern_equivalent: EVALUATE TRUE / WHEN condition / PERFORM paragraph. Or simply use a flag variable and IF/ELSE.
entries.example:
ALTER P-030 TO PROCEED TO P-040.
...
P-030.
    GO TO P-040.  *> Target changed by ALTER above
entries.risk: Extremely difficult to trace statically. The GO TO target is invisible in the source — you must simulate ALTER execution to know where it goes. Deprecated in COBOL-85, removed in COBOL-2002.

entries.name: GO TO
entries.purpose: Unconditional jump to a named paragraph. Transfers control without returning.
entries.mainframe_context: COBOL-68 had limited structured programming. GO TO was the primary flow control mechanism alongside PERFORM.
entries.modern_equivalent: PERFORM (structured call with return), inline PERFORM blocks, or EVALUATE/WHEN.
entries.example:
GO TO PROCESS-NEXT-RECORD.
...
PROCESS-NEXT-RECORD.
    READ ...
entries.risk: Creates 'spaghetti code' — tangled control flow that's hard to follow. GO TO chains across multiple paragraphs are nearly impossible to trace mentally.

entries.name: PERFORM THRU
entries.purpose: Execute a range of paragraphs as a single unit. PERFORM A THRU B executes A, then all paragraphs between A and B, then B.
entries.mainframe_context: Before inline PERFORM, THRU was the way to group related paragraphs. Common in tax calculations, report formatting, and batch processing.
entries.modern_equivalent: Inline PERFORM blocks, or simply PERFORM each paragraph individually.
entries.example:
PERFORM COMPUTE-FED THRU COMPUTE-EXIT.
...
COMPUTE-FED.
    ...
COMPUTE-STATE.
    ...
COMPUTE-EXIT.
    EXIT.
entries.risk: Inserting a new paragraph between the start and end of the range SILENTLY adds it to execution. No compiler warning. This is one of the most dangerous COBOL patterns.

entries.name: Nested IF without END-IF
entries.purpose: Multiple IF statements terminated by a single period. Each ELSE matches the nearest unmatched IF.
entries.mainframe_context: COBOL-68 had no END-IF. A period (.) terminated ALL open IF scopes simultaneously. Indentation was cosmetic only.
entries.modern_equivalent: IF/ELSE/END-IF blocks, or EVALUATE/WHEN for multi-way branching.
entries.example:
IF A > 10
    IF B > 20
        MOVE 1 TO C
    ELSE
        MOVE 2 TO C.  *> Period ends BOTH IFs
entries.risk: Adding a line in the wrong place changes which IF each ELSE matches. A misplaced period terminates all open scopes. These bugs are nearly invisible in code review.

entries.name: Paragraph fall-through
entries.purpose: When a paragraph ends without GO TO or STOP RUN, execution continues into the next paragraph sequentially.
entries.mainframe_context: COBOL paragraphs are not functions — they're labeled sections of sequential code. Fall-through is the default behavior.
entries.modern_equivalent: Explicit PERFORM calls. Each paragraph should end with a GO TO, STOP RUN, or EXIT (or be called via PERFORM which returns automatically).
entries.example:
PARA-A.
    MOVE 1 TO X.
*> Falls through to PARA-B
PARA-B.
    MOVE 2 TO Y.
entries.risk: Unintentional fall-through can cause paragraphs to execute when they shouldn't. Adding a new paragraph between two existing ones changes the fall-through chain.

entries.name: COMP-3
entries.purpose: Packed decimal storage. Each byte holds 2 decimal digits. The last nibble is the sign (C=positive, D=negative, F=unsigned).
entries.mainframe_context: IBM System/360+ has hardware instructions (AP, SP, MP, DP) that operate directly on packed decimal. No conversion needed — the CPU arithmetic unit works natively with this format. 50% denser than DISPLAY for numeric data.
entries.modern_equivalent: No direct equivalent. Modern CPUs use IEEE 754 floating-point. Java's BigDecimal and Python's decimal.Decimal are the closest in terms of exact decimal arithmetic.
entries.example:
05  SALARY  PIC S9(7)V99 COMP-3.
*> 9 digits → ceil((9+1)/2) = 5 bytes
*> Value 75000.00 stored as: 07 50 00 00 0C
entries.risk: COMP-3 fields contain binary data that can't be displayed or printed directly. Hex dumps are needed to read them. LINE SEQUENTIAL files cannot contain COMP-3 data.

entries.name: COMP
entries.purpose: Binary integer storage. PIC S9(4) COMP = 2-byte halfword. PIC S9(9) COMP = 4-byte fullword.
entries.mainframe_context: IBM mainframes have fast binary arithmetic (AR, SR, MR, DR). COMP fields are used for counters, indexes, and integer operations where speed matters more than decimal precision.
entries.modern_equivalent: int / long in any modern language.
entries.example:
05  HOURS-WORKED  PIC S9(4) COMP.
*> 2 bytes, range -32768 to +32767
entries.risk: Mixing COMP and COMP-3 in arithmetic causes implicit conversions that add CPU overhead. On batch runs processing millions of records, this matters.

entries.name: COPY REPLACING
entries.purpose: Include a copybook but substitute specific text. COPY 'RECORD.cpy' REPLACING ==OLD-PREFIX== BY ==NEW-PREFIX==.
entries.mainframe_context: Allows reusing record layouts across programs that name fields differently. A single copybook can serve multiple programs.
entries.modern_equivalent: Generics, templates, or simply renaming after import.
entries.example:
COPY 'EMPREC.cpy' REPLACING
    ==EMP-== BY ==WS-EMP-==.
entries.risk: REPLACING operates on text, not identifiers. It can match inside comments, strings, or partial names, causing unexpected substitutions.

entries.name: 88-level condition
entries.purpose: Named boolean test on a field's value. 88 ACCT-ACTIVE VALUE 'A'. Makes IF ACCT-ACTIVE equivalent to IF ACCT-STATUS = 'A'.
entries.mainframe_context: Self-documenting code pattern. Instead of IF ACCT-STATUS = 'A', you write IF ACCT-ACTIVE. The 88-level both documents valid values and makes code more readable.
entries.modern_equivalent: Enum values with named constants, or boolean properties.
entries.example:
05  STATUS  PIC X(1).
    88  IS-ACTIVE    VALUE 'A'.
    88  IS-CLOSED    VALUE 'C'.
...
IF IS-ACTIVE
    PERFORM PROCESS-ACCOUNT.
entries.risk: None — this is one of COBOL's best features. Universally recommended.

entries.name: REDEFINES
entries.purpose: Overlay one data item on top of another, sharing the same memory. Different 'views' of the same bytes.
entries.mainframe_context: Memory was expensive. REDEFINES allowed multiple interpretations of the same storage area — e.g., a date field viewed as 8 individual digits or as 4 two-digit groups.
entries.modern_equivalent: Union types in C, or struct reinterpretation. Python's struct.unpack with different format strings.
entries.example:
05  DATE-FULL   PIC 9(8).
05  DATE-PARTS REDEFINES DATE-FULL.
    10  DATE-YYYY  PIC 9(4).
    10  DATE-MM    PIC 9(2).
    10  DATE-DD    PIC 9(2).
entries.risk: Changing one view changes all views. If DATE-FULL is 20260227, DATE-YYYY is 2026. But writing 99 to DATE-MM makes DATE-FULL contain 20269927 — corrupting the year.

entries.name: Implied decimal (V)
entries.purpose: V marks where the decimal point is, but no actual '.' is stored. PIC 9(5)V99 stores 7 digits; the V tells COBOL that the last 2 are cents.
entries.mainframe_context: Fixed-point arithmetic avoids floating-point rounding errors. Financial systems require exact decimal math — $10.00 + $10.00 must equal $20.00, never $19.999999.
entries.modern_equivalent: decimal.Decimal in Python, BigDecimal in Java, or integer-cents representation (store $10.00 as 1000).
entries.example:
05  BALANCE  PIC S9(10)V99.
*> 12 bytes. Value 12345.67 stored as '000001234567'
entries.risk: If you MOVE a V99 field to a field without V, the decimal position is lost. COBOL doesn't warn — it just truncates or pads.

entries.name: JCL job card
entries.purpose: Job Control Language (JCL) tells the mainframe operating system (z/OS) what program to run, what files to use, and what resources to allocate.
entries.mainframe_context: On mainframes, you don't 'run a program' — you submit a JOB. The JOB card specifies accounting info, job name, priority class, and output routing. EXEC runs the program. DD statements define file assignments.
entries.modern_equivalent: Docker Compose, Kubernetes manifests, or shell scripts that configure environment before running a program.
entries.risk: JCL syntax errors prevent the job from running. A missing comma or wrong DSN can take down a batch run.

entries.name: VSAM
entries.purpose: Virtual Storage Access Method — IBM's high-performance file system for mainframes. Supports keyed (KSDS), sequential (ESDS), and relative (RRDS) datasets.
entries.mainframe_context: VSAM replaced older access methods (ISAM, BSAM). It provides indexed access, buffering, and concurrent access control. Most COBOL batch programs read/write VSAM files.
entries.modern_equivalent: Database tables with indexes. SQLite, PostgreSQL, etc.
entries.example:
SELECT ACCOUNT-FILE ASSIGN TO ACCTFILE
    ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
    ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC
    RECORD KEY IS ACCT-ID.
entries.risk: VSAM files can become fragmented, requiring periodic reorganization (IDCAMS REPRO). CI/CA splits degrade performance.

entries.name: CICS
entries.purpose: Customer Information Control System — IBM's online transaction processing (OLTP) monitor. Runs COBOL programs interactively (not batch).
entries.mainframe_context: CICS handles terminal I/O, transaction management, and concurrency for online COBOL programs (e.g., bank teller screens, airline reservation systems).
entries.modern_equivalent: Application server (Tomcat, Express.js) + REST API framework.
entries.example:
EXEC CICS SEND MAP('ACCTMAP')
    FROM(ACCT-DISPLAY-AREA)
    MAPSET('ACCTSET')
END-EXEC.
entries.risk: CICS programs share memory — a bug in one program can crash others. ABEND codes (like ASRA) indicate specific failure types.

entries.name: ABEND codes
entries.purpose: Abnormal End codes identify why a mainframe program crashed. System ABENDs start with S (e.g., S0C7 = data exception). User ABENDs start with U.
entries.mainframe_context: When a COBOL program ABENDs, the system produces a dump with the ABEND code. S0C7 (data exception) is the most common — it means a non-numeric character was found in a numeric field during arithmetic.
entries.modern_equivalent: Exception types (NullPointerException, ValueError) with stack traces.
entries.example:
*> S0C7: Tried to add 'ABC' to a COMP-3 field
*> S0C4: Tried to access memory outside program area
*> U4038: Application-defined error (custom ABEND)
entries.risk: ABENDs in batch runs can leave files in inconsistent states if COMMIT/ROLLBACK isn't properly handled.

entries.name: Dead code
entries.purpose: Code that can never execute because no PERFORM, GO TO, or fall-through reaches it.
entries.mainframe_context: Removing code from production COBOL requires a change request, regression testing, and sign-off. It's universally considered 'safer' to leave dead code in place. Over decades, dead paragraphs accumulate.
entries.modern_equivalent: Delete the code. Version control preserves the history.
entries.example:
*> This paragraph is never called:
DEAD-PARA.
    COMPUTE X = Y * Z.
    DISPLAY 'THIS NEVER RUNS'.
entries.risk: Dead code misleads readers into thinking it executes. It makes the program longer and harder to understand.

entries.name: Misleading comments
entries.purpose: Comments that describe what the code USED to do, not what it currently does.
entries.mainframe_context: Comments are not verified by the compiler. When code changes but comments don't, the comments become lies. This is the #1 documentation bug in legacy COBOL.
entries.modern_equivalent: Self-documenting code with meaningful variable names. Comments should explain WHY, not WHAT.
entries.example:
*> PMR: 'Apply standard 5% state tax'
COMPUTE TAX = GROSS * 0.0725.  *> Actually 7.25%!
entries.risk: New developers trust comments over code. 'Fixing' the code to match the comments introduces bugs.

entries.name: Magic numbers
entries.purpose: Unnamed numeric literals scattered throughout code. COMPUTE PAY = HOURS * 40 — what does 40 mean?
entries.mainframe_context: Early COBOL had limited naming conventions. Numeric literals were used for 'obvious' values like 40 hours/week or 1.5x overtime. But they're only obvious to the original developer.
entries.modern_equivalent: Named constants: STANDARD-HOURS = 40, OT-MULTIPLIER = 1.50.
entries.example:
IF HOURS > 40
    COMPUTE OT = (HOURS - 40) * RATE * 1.50.
entries.risk: When the value changes, you must find every occurrence. Searching for '40' returns hundreds of false positives.

entries.name: Mixed COMP types
entries.purpose: Using COMP-3, COMP, and DISPLAY in the same arithmetic expression. Each requires implicit type conversion.
entries.mainframe_context: Different developers preferred different USAGE types. The compiler handles conversions silently, so there's no immediate error. But each conversion adds CPU instructions.
entries.modern_equivalent: Consistent typing. In Python, don't mix int and Decimal in the same expression without explicit conversion.
entries.example:
ADD MED-COST (COMP-3) TO DENTAL-COST (COMP)
    GIVING TOTAL (DISPLAY).
*> Two implicit conversions per ADD
entries.risk: Performance degradation in batch runs. On millions of records, implicit conversions add measurable CPU time.

entries.name: Y2K artifacts
entries.purpose: Code added during Y2K remediation that was never cleaned up: parallel 2-digit and 4-digit date fields, windowing techniques, validation routines.
entries.mainframe_context: Y2K was done under extreme time pressure with massive budgets. The strategy was 'add new fields, keep old fields, ship it.' Nobody went back to clean up.
entries.modern_equivalent: N/A — just use 4-digit years from the start.
entries.example:
05  DATE-FULL  PIC 9(8).  *> Y2K: new
05  DATE-SHORT PIC 9(6).  *> Pre-Y2K: old
*> Both are populated. Both are written. Neither is removed.
entries.risk: Y2K windowing (YY >= 50 → 19XX) will break again in 2050. Dead Y2K validation code wastes cycles.

entries.name: MOVE truncation
entries.purpose: COBOL MOVE silently truncates data that does not fit the receiving field. Alphanumeric MOVEs are left-justified and right-truncated; numeric MOVEs are right-justified and left-truncated.
entries.mainframe_context: Fixed-length fields are fundamental to COBOL. Every field has an exact byte size defined by its PIC clause. MOVE does not raise errors -- it truncates to fit. MOVE CORRESPONDING matches fields by name between groups; renaming a field silently drops it.
entries.modern_equivalent: Type-safe assignment with explicit casting. Runtime exceptions on overflow.
entries.example:
MOVE 1000005 TO PIC-FIELD.
*> PIC 9(6) stores 000005 -- the leading 1 vanishes
*> PIC X(4) receiving "COBOL" stores "COBO"
entries.risk: High-order digits vanish without any error or warning. Group MOVEs lose decimal alignment because they are treated as alphanumeric regardless of subordinate types.

entries.name: EBCDIC collating sequence
entries.purpose: EBCDIC sorts lowercase before uppercase before digits: a < A < 1. ASCII reverses this: 1 < A < a.
entries.mainframe_context: IBM mainframes use EBCDIC natively. All SORT operations, SEARCH ALL binary searches, and IF comparisons use the platform collating sequence. Programs migrated from z/OS to ASCII platforms produce different sort orders.
entries.modern_equivalent: Unicode with locale-aware collation (Collator in Java, locale.strxfrm in Python).
entries.example:
SORT SORT-FILE ON ASCENDING KEY MERCH-ID.
*> EBCDIC: "abc" < "ABC" < "123"
*> ASCII:  "123" < "ABC" < "abc"
entries.risk: SEARCH ALL on a table sorted in EBCDIC order produces wrong results on ASCII. Every missed instance is a silent migration bug.

entries.name: FILE STATUS codes
entries.purpose: Two-character code returned after every file I/O operation. Must be PIC X(2) not PIC 99.
entries.mainframe_context: The five codes every banking COBOL programmer memorizes: 00=success, 10=end of file, 22=duplicate key, 23=record not found, 35=file not found at OPEN (typically missing DD in JCL).
entries.modern_equivalent: Exception handling with specific exception types (FileNotFoundError, DuplicateKeyError).
entries.example:
SELECT ACCT-FILE ASSIGN TO "ACCOUNTS.DAT"
    FILE STATUS IS WS-FS.
...
READ ACCT-FILE.
IF WS-FS NOT = "00" DISPLAY "FAIL: " WS-FS.
entries.risk: Unchecked FILE STATUS means errors propagate silently. A failed READ returns stale data from the previous successful read.

entries.name: Overpunch sign encoding
entries.purpose: Signed DISPLAY fields encode the sign in the zone nibble of the last byte. -123 in PIC S9(3) displays as '12L'.
entries.mainframe_context: In EBCDIC: positive 0-9 map to {,A-I (zone C); negatives to },J-R (zone D). ASCII Micro Focus uses 0x70 zone (p-y). A character translation CORRUPTS signed fields.
entries.modern_equivalent: Explicit sign representation (negative numbers have a - prefix).
entries.example:
*> PIC S9(3) DISPLAY format:
*>   +123 stored as hex F1 F2 C3
*>   -123 stored as hex F1 F2 D3 (displays as '12L')
entries.risk: Python parsers must handle overpunch explicitly or every negative value becomes garbage.

entries.name: SPACES vs LOW-VALUES vs HIGH-VALUES
entries.purpose: Figurative constants with specific hex values. SPACES=X'40'(EBCDIC)/X'20'(ASCII). LOW-VALUES=X'00'. HIGH-VALUES=X'FF'.
entries.mainframe_context: LOW-VALUES is the minimum collating value. HIGH-VALUES is the maximum. A field initialized to LOW-VALUES does NOT equal SPACES.
entries.modern_equivalent: null/None (LOW-VALUES), empty string (SPACES), MAX_VALUE sentinel (HIGH-VALUES).
entries.example:
MOVE LOW-VALUES TO WS-RECORD.
IF WS-RECORD = SPACES  *> FALSE!
entries.risk: Moving LOW-VALUES to a numeric field then performing arithmetic triggers S0C7 data exception abend.

entries.name: Working-Storage persistence
entries.purpose: In batch, Working-Storage persists throughout execution. In CICS, each task gets a fresh copy.
entries.mainframe_context: CICS pseudo-conversational: between interactions, the program ends and restarts. WS resets. State goes through COMMAREA (max 32,763 bytes) or Channels/Containers (CICS TS 3.1+).
entries.modern_equivalent: Session state in web applications (cookies, session storage, database-backed sessions).
entries.example:
*> BATCH: WS-COUNTER persists across records
*> CICS:  WS-COUNTER resets between transactions
entries.risk: Converting batch to online without handling WS persistence leaves stale values. The #1 source of intermittent bugs in online COBOL.

entries.name: Copybook dependency chains
entries.purpose: Copybooks are the schema. Changing a field requires recompiling ALL including programs -- miss one and you get silent field misalignment.
entries.mainframe_context: One change can affect 50-200 programs. No dependency tracking beyond grep and tribal knowledge. Over 70% of business rules in legacy systems exist only in the code.
entries.modern_equivalent: Schema migration tools (Flyway, Alembic) with automated dependency tracking.
entries.example:
COPY "EMPREC.cpy".
*> Used by: PAYROLL, TAXCALC, DEDUCTN, PAYBATCH
*> Change EMP-SALARY PIC -> recompile ALL FOUR
entries.risk: Programs with stale copybook versions read data with wrong field boundaries. Wrong numbers that look plausible.

entries.name: Y2K windowing
entries.purpose: Interprets 2-digit years using a pivot: IF YY >= 40 THEN 19XX ELSE 20XX. The pivot determines the cutoff.
entries.mainframe_context: Full conversion was too expensive. Windowing deferred the problem ~50 years. IBM YEARWINDOW compiler option. Windows are now expiring.
entries.modern_equivalent: Use 4-digit years. The Y2K problem was deferred, not solved.
entries.example:
IF WS-YY >= 50
    MOVE 19 TO WS-CC
ELSE
    MOVE 20 TO WS-CC.
*> 2050 is interpreted as 1950!
entries.risk: 30-year mortgages from 2020 mature in 2050, crossing the pivot boundary. Practitioners report already encountering date problems.

entries.name: Fixed-point arithmetic (banking)
entries.purpose: COMP-3 stores exact decimal values. 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.3 exactly, unlike IEEE 754.
entries.mainframe_context: IBM z-Series has native BCD instructions making COMP-3 ~7-10x faster than binary. PIC S9(13)V99 COMP-3 = 8 bytes, up to +/-$999 trillion. The banking standard.
entries.modern_equivalent: Python decimal.Decimal, Java BigDecimal, or integer-cents representation.
entries.example:
05 AMOUNT PIC S9(13)V99 COMP-3.
*> 8 bytes, exact decimal, no IEEE 754 errors
entries.risk: Using COMP-1/COMP-2 (floating point) for financial amounts introduces representation errors.

entries.name: Day-count conventions
entries.purpose: Methods for counting days in interest: 30/360 (bonds), Actual/360 (money markets), Actual/365 (UK), Actual/Actual (Treasuries).
entries.mainframe_context: 30/360 assumes 30-day months. Actual/360 uses actual days over 360-day year -- charges ~5 extra days annually. All arithmetic uses INTEGER-OF-DATE and COMP-3.
entries.modern_equivalent: Date libraries with explicit day-count parameters (QuantLib, Apache Commons DayCount).
entries.example:
*> 30/360: Feb has 30 days
*> Actual/360: Feb has 28/29 days, denom 360
*> Difference on $1M at 5%: ~$694/year
entries.risk: Wrong convention on a 30-year mortgage changes total interest by thousands of dollars.

entries.name: Regulatory compliance (CTR/SAR)
entries.purpose: Mandatory reporting: CTR for cash > $10K, SAR for structuring patterns, OFAC for sanctions screening.
entries.mainframe_context: Nightly batch detects: CTR (same-day cash > $10K, filed within 15 days), SAR (sub-$10K structuring, velocity anomalies, filed within 30 days), OFAC (SDN list fuzzy matching). SWIFT MT103/MT202/MT940 transitioning to ISO 20022.
entries.modern_equivalent: Automated compliance engines with real-time screening and regulatory reporting pipelines.
entries.example:
*> CTR: IF DAILY-CASH > 10000
*> SAR: IF TXN-COUNT > 5 AND EACH < 10000
*> OFAC: fuzzy match against SDN list
entries.risk: Missing a CTR/SAR filing is a federal crime. The $10K threshold unchanged since 1970.

entries.name: DB2 embedded SQL
entries.purpose: EXEC SQL...END-EXEC embeds SQL in COBOL. Host variables prefixed with : pass data between COBOL and DB2.
entries.mainframe_context: DCLGEN generates copybooks from DB2 tables. Indicator variables (PIC S9(4) COMP) signal NULLs (-1). SQLCA codes: 0=success, +100=not found, -803=duplicate, -811=multiple rows. In CICS, use SYNCPOINT for commits.
entries.modern_equivalent: ORM (SQLAlchemy, Hibernate) or prepared statements with parameterized queries.
entries.example:
EXEC SQL
    SELECT NAME INTO :WS-NAME
    FROM ACCOUNTS WHERE ID = :WS-ID
END-EXEC.
IF SQLCODE = +100 DISPLAY "NOT FOUND".
entries.risk: EXEC SQL is the primary migration blocker from IBM to GnuCOBOL.

entries.name: GnuCOBOL compatibility
entries.purpose: Open-source COBOL compiler passing 9,700+ of 9,748 NIST tests. Translates COBOL to C to native binary via GCC.
entries.mainframe_context: Dialect flags: -std=ibm, -std=mf, -std=cobol2014. COMP-3 byte-identical (critical win). COMP-1/COMP-2 incompatible (hex float vs IEEE 754). EXEC CICS requires middleware replacement.
entries.modern_equivalent: Cross-compilation tools, compatibility shims, platform abstraction layers.
entries.example:
cobc -x -free -std=ibm -I copybooks PAYROLL.cob
*> Produces native executable via GCC
entries.risk: EXEC CICS not supported. EBCDIC-to-ASCII corrupts signed DISPLAY. Hex literals change meaning. JCL must become shell scripts.
